```{r cross-tab, echo=FALSE}
    "embedding geometry separates the four intellectual domains."),
  col.names = c("Topic label", "Darwin", "Smith", "Holmes", "Plato"),
  booktabs  = TRUE,
  align     = c("l", "r", "r", "r", "r")
)
```

The topic labels—recovered automatically from c-TF-IDF scores—match the
intellectual domains of the source texts.  Noise paragraphs (those HDBSCAN
could not assign to any cluster) are typically short transitional passages or
chapter headings that lack sufficient topical content.  Together, the
high within-topic purity and interpretable term lists confirm that the
\pkg{Rhobots} pipeline successfully recovers meaningful latent structure from
real-world heterogeneous text.

# Discussion {#sec:discussion}

## Comparison with Existing R Topic Models

Table \@ref(tab:comparison) summarises the main differences between
\pkg{Rhobots} and established R packages for topic modeling.

```{r comparison, echo=FALSE}
comp <- data.frame(
  Package    = c("\\CRANpkg{topicmodels}", "\\CRANpkg{stm}", "\\CRANpkg{keyATM}",
                 "\\pkg{Rhobots}"),
  Model      = c("LDA", "STM", "Keyword-assisted LDA", "BERTopic"),
  Embeddings = c("No", "No", "No", "Yes (BERT)"),
  `Nr topics` = c("Fixed", "Fixed", "Fixed", "Automatic"),
  `Python dep.` = c("No", "No", "No", "No"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE,
  check.names = FALSE
)
knitr::kable(
  comp,
  caption  = "Comparison of R topic modeling packages.",
  booktabs = TRUE,
  escape   = FALSE
)
```

LDA \citep{blei2003lda} and its extensions, including the Structural Topic
Model \citep{roberts2019stm} and keyword-assisted variants
\citep{eshima2023keyatm}, have well-studied statistical properties and scale
well to very large corpora through online or collapsed Gibbs sampling.  They
remain the right choice when probabilistic topic proportions per document are
required for downstream regression, or when the corpus is so large that
transformer inference would be prohibitively slow.  \pkg{Rhobots} is better
suited to corpora where semantic coherence of topics is paramount and where
the number of topics is not known in advance.

## Domain-Specific Encoders

The quality of the topic model depends substantially on the quality of the
sentence embeddings.  A generic model such as
`sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2` is fast and adequate for many
general-purpose corpora.  For scientometric or patent analysis, domain-adapted
encoders such as `allenai/scibert_scivocab_uncased`,
`pritamdeka/S-Scibert-snli-multinli-stsb`, or `anferico/bert-for-patents`
typically produce more coherent topics.  \pkg{Rhobots} is model-agnostic:
`load_hf_bert()` accepts any BERT-family architecture available on the
HuggingFace Hub, and the `weights_path` argument allows pinning to a locally
converted safetensors file.

## Limitations

Several limitations should be noted.  First, transformer inference is slower
than LDA on CPU; for corpora of more than 100,000 documents, embedding time
can be a bottleneck.  The embedding cache (`embed_texts_cached()`) mitigates
this for iterative analyses, and GPU acceleration is available when \pkg{torch}
is installed with CUDA support.  Second, HDBSCAN can be sensitive to its
`min_pts` hyperparameter; too-small values produce many small, noisy topics
while too-large values merge semantically distinct themes.  Cross-validation
on topic coherence metrics is advisable.  Third, while \pkg{Rhobots} provides POS-filtered and C-value representation
models as well as LLM-based post-hoc label generation, it does not yet offer a
native KeyBERT-inspired term selector (which ranks terms by their semantic
similarity to the topic centroid rather than by class TF-IDF) or a BERT-score
re-ranker.  These remain natural areas for future development.

## Reproducibility

UMAP contains a stochastic component.  \pkg{Rhobots} accepts a `seed`
argument that is passed to `uwot::umap()` and `set.seed()` before clustering.
Setting the seed ensures that two runs on the same data produce identical
topic assignments.  PCA and agglomerative clustering are fully deterministic
regardless of seed.

# Conclusion {#sec:conclusion}

\pkg{Rhobots} brings the BERTopic algorithm to R as a self-contained,
dependency-light package.  By replacing the Python runtime with native R
packages for every stage of the pipeline, it removes a significant barrier
to adoption in teaching, rapid prototyping, and reproducible research settings.
The modular S3 architecture makes it straightforward to experiment with
alternative reduction and clustering algorithms, and the accessor API ensures
a smooth transition for users already familiar with the Python package.

The package is available on GitHub at \url{https://github.com/JPvdP/Rhobots}
and can be installed with:

```{r install, eval=FALSE}
pak::pak("JPvdP/Rhobots")
```

## Acknowledgements {-}

The algorithm and API design are based entirely on the Python BERTopic package
by \citet{grootendorst2022bertopic}.  We gratefully acknowledge the work of
Maarten Grootendorst and every contributor to the BERTopic project.
This package was developed for the \emph{Data Analytics for Sustainability}
course at Utrecht University, Faculty of Geosciences.

All analyses were performed in R \citep{R}.
